title: Left, Right, East, West: Pope Leo's AI Encyclical Found One Register
subtitle: Nearly every outlet alarmed. Germany, champion of the EU AI Act, was absent. Mother Jones stood alone.
body_md:
Pope Leo XIV published his first encyclical, 'Magnifica Humanitas,' calling for robust AI regulation, rejecting AI in lethal decisions, and declaring the just war theory outdated. Across 27 outlets and 15 countries, the response was unusual: left and right, secular and religious, European and Asian press converged on the same alarm register. France's Le Monde and Le Figaro — typically on opposite editorial sides — both published the same warning vocabulary. The Straits Times and Mother Jones, a Southeast Asian broadsheet and a US progressive magazine, landed in the same frame.

[AFP / France 24](https://www.france24.com/en/europe/20260525-pope-leo-xiv-ai-manifesto-ethical-risks-global-impact) headlined it as a call for "'robust' regulation of AI in sweeping manifesto." [ANSA English](https://www.ansa.it/english/news/vatican/2026/05/25/popes-first-encyclical-magnifica-humanitas-presented-in-vatican_54cfc96f-f4d1-4339-a1ad-b05477b4e04d.html) quoted the encyclical's core demand: technology "should advance without causing the human heart to regress." [Globe and Mail](https://www.theglobeandmail.com/business/article-pope-leo-regulation-ai-manifesto/) pulled the structural sentence: "robust legal frameworks, independent oversight, informed users and a political system that does not abdicate its responsibility are required." Across European outlets, this governance-architecture frame was dominant. French coverage coalesced specifically around the verb Leo used — 'désarmer' (disarm AI) — which [BFM TV](https://www.bfmtv.com/tech/intelligence-artificielle/humanite-magnifique-le-pape-leon-xiv-veut-desarmer-l-ia-pour-l-empecher-de-dominer-l-humain-et-appelle-a-creer-un-code-ethique-commun_AD-202605250447.html) quoted alongside his denunciation of "nouvelles formes d'esclavage" (new forms of slavery) in AI's shadow.

The editorial geography split at the political layer. Non-Western outlets — [The Hindu Intl](https://www.thehindu.com/news/international/pope-calls-idea-of-just-war-put-forward-by-us-outdated-in-first-manifesto/article71020728.ece) and [Middle East Eye](https://www.middleeasteye.net/live-blog/live-blog-update/pope-calls-us-backed-just-war-idea-outdated) — led not on AI governance but on the encyclical's direct rebuke of Trump's just war doctrine, which Leo labelled "outdated" and warned was sliding toward "a violent culture of power." The Hindu quoted Trump's response — calling Leo "WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy" — and noted that Catholic-convert VP JD Vance had urged the Pope to be careful on theological matters. This Vatican-vs.-Trump angle dominated South and Southeast Asian headlines while European and religious press treated it as secondary to the AI governance architecture.

[Straits Times](https://www.straitstimes.com/world/bold-decisions-key-points-from-popes-ai-manifesto) distilled the economic stakes: "Without bold decisions, the prospect of greater poverty and inequality looms large, which would leave many individuals marginalised, stranded and surrounded by the machines and automated systems that have replaced them." [Politico Europe](https://www.politico.eu/article/the-vatican-vs-killer-robots-pope-leo-xiv-takes-aim-at-ai-warfare/) extracted the weapons line cleanly: AI should never be used to make lethal decisions, chatbots should never be equated with humans. [Malay Mail World](https://www.malaymail.com/news/world/2026/05/25/pope-urges-disarming-of-artificial-intelligence-in-major-manifesto-says-it-needs-to-be-human-friendly/221376) noted that Anthropic — the Vatican's co-presenter at the encyclical launch — is embroiled in a US military legal dispute over lethal autonomous warfare, contextualising the company's presence at the Vatican within its Pentagon conflict.

Two outlier findings are worth separating from the consensus. [El Pais](https://elpais.com/sociedad/2026-05-25/el-papa-leon-xiv-lanza-con-su-primera-enciclica-una-llamada-de-alarma-contra-el-tecnofascismo-quien-controla-la-ia-impondra-su-vision-moral.html) was the sole outlet to use the term "tecnofascismo," escalating the power-concentration frame from regulatory concern to political authoritarianism: "quien controla la IA impondrá su visión moral." Every other outlet in the corpus used variants of "governance" or "regulation." [Mother Jones](https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2026/05/pope-leo-artificial-intelligence-letter-encyclical/) stood alone in raising a conflict-of-interest flag on Anthropic's Vatican platform: "the relationship between the Vatican and Anthropic poses risks of laundering the AI company" — a skeptical note entirely absent from all other coverage, including outlets editorially skeptical of both the Church and Silicon Valley.

The most significant editorial absence in the corpus is Germany. The country that championed the EU AI Act and hosts Europe's largest Catholic population produced no outlet coverage in this cluster. German press likely prioritized EU governance disputes or Ukraine-related news — but the EU's chief regulatory voice was missing from a debate it formally shaped.

[Religion News Service](https://religionnews.com/2026/05/25/in-magnifica-humanitas-pope-leo-delivers-on-a-people-first-vision-for-ai/) offered the only US-specific grounding of the encyclical's reception: AI speakers at American commencement ceremonies were "met with boos and groans from graduates," situating papal doctrine within documented popular American unease. It was the only outlet to invoke Rerum Novarum extensively, placing 'Magnifica Humanitas' within Catholic social doctrine's long response to economic transformation.

The convergence across this corpus is itself the story. When an encyclical produces identical alarm across outlets that otherwise occupy opposite editorial universes, what the document said matters less than what its reception reveals: a cross-ideological consensus that the current pace of AI deployment is outrunning the political capacity to govern it.
